evidence: The foremost aim of the Casebook Virtual Patient application is to help to teach clinical diagnostic reasoning and decision-making skills to undergraduate students of medicine through the examination of real patient cases.
Casebook emphasises the use of pre-existing electronic health record data as the basis for case creation.
This experience builds pattern recognition, meaning the more experience a student has, the more likely they will make a correct diagnosis from the data available to them.

Learning theory research has shown that students learn decision-making skills best when they have access to multitudes of patient cases and it is this plurality that allows students to develop their illness scripts effectively.
novice learners, such as medical students, have limited clinical experience and therefore need to approach most consultations in a more analytical ('hypothetico-deductive') way.
A proposed strategy for teaching clinical reasoning is therefore to teach it within a skill framework in the clinical consultation room.

the idea of a ‘coach’ to assist the student during an evolving consultation is highlighted as essential to monitor the student’s questions and responses and commenting on their relevance and accuracy with instant feedback during the consultation.
Teaching clinical reasoning encourages personal reflection and refinement of the clinician’s own clinical reasoning skills to improve patient outcomes.

Half of all participants were instructed to reason in a combined manner and half were given no explicit instruction regarding the diagnostic task.
the instruction to utilise a combined reasoning approach resulted in greater diagnostic accuracy relative to leaving students to their own intuitions regarding how best to approach new cases.
the effect was particularly pronounced when cases were made challenging by biasing participants towards an incorrect diagnosis.

explicit instruction to utilise both analytic and non-analytic forms of diagnostic reasoning can improve novice clinicians’ diagnostic accuracy.
Greater diagnostic accuracy was achieved following both contrastive learning and instructions to use a combined reasoning strategy.

Completing the table presented on the answer sheet forced the consideration of alternative diagnoses and of findings that conflicted with a suspected diagnosis.

Schema-based instruction may alter knowledge organisation and diagnostic reasoning strategies through the provision of structured knowledge to novice trainees.
the provision of schemas is hypothesised to aid in the development of compiled knowledge because schemas present the information in a structured, clinically relevant way.
This study provides important proof-of-concept for a schema-based approach and suggests there is substantial benefit to using this approach with novice trainees.
Students receiving schema-based instruction performed better on structured knowledge questions and diagnostic success was higher in the schema-based instruction group for taught lesions.

students in the self-explanation condition, compared with those in the control condition, demonstrated better diagnostic performance on subsequent clinical cases, but this effect emerged only for cases concerning the less familiar topic.
self-explanation is an instructional procedure, the positive effects of which on learning have been demonstrated in a variety of domains.
self-explanation has been shown to compel learners to attend to the material in a meaningful way, triggering several key cognitive processes.
self-explanation may have a greater effect on learning as students will generate more inferences and new links, with greater integration of new information into their prior knowledge.

undergraduate primary care rotations are often short. This means that students are unlikely to observe consultations or meet patients suffering from all of the core curricular presentations to primary care.
VP cases can be used to compress time, allowing students to observe strategies such as ‘test of time’ or ‘test of treatment’.
users develop enhanced cognitive and meta-cognitive processing when using video-enriched cases compared to text alone.
the advantages of using video cases to educate non-experts in diagnostic strategies for paediatrics were recently highlighted.

active processing of these examples appears to be critical to their learning from them
Group 1 showed a signiﬁcantly higher diagnostic performance score after 1 week than the control group
students feel unprepared for clinical decision making as FY1s, and lack effective decision making strategies.
A teaching intervention was developed, consisting of a clinical decision making tool, a tutorial and scenarios within a simulated ward environment.
A tripartite teaching intervention was developed. Analytical, non-analytical and combined approaches to decision making were incorporated in the intervention.
Experiencing complex decision making scenarios through individually orientated simulation results in students being subjectively more prepared for work as FY1s.
A teaching intervention, including simulated individual clinical scenarios, later in undergraduate training, appeared to be useful in improving medical students’ decision making.
self-explanation while solving clinical cases may be an effective strategy to foster reasoning in clinical clerks dealing with less familiar cases.
self-explanation segments from less familiar cases... stimulate medical students to engage in more extensive thinking during self-explanation.
this may clarify the previously found positive effect that self-explanation has on the diagnosis of unfamiliar cases.
Students provided more self-explanation segments from less familiar cases.
less familiar cases seem to activate students’ biomedical knowledge, which in turn helps them to create new links between biomedical and clinical knowledge.
Cognitive forcing strategies (CFS) may reduce error arising from cognitive biases.
the educational interventions suggested by experts in clinical reasoning and employed in our study to teach CFS failed to show any reduction in diagnostic error by novices.

students with low knowledge are enabled to learn more effectively when placed in real-life scenarios.
real-life scenarios can increase cognitive load.
increased cognitive load can lead to negative learning outcomes.
A serious game was developed to train surgical residents in clinical decision-making regarding biliary tract disease.
The serious game contains cases on diagnosis and management of patients with biliary tract disease.
Questionnaire results showed that educators and surgical trainees found the game both realistic and useful for surgical training.
This serious game showed clear discriminatory ability between different levels of expertise in biliary tract disease management and clear teaching capability.
